name: Bhumyamalaki
usesInAyurveda: Tridoshic Modulation: Prominently pacifies Pitta and Kapha doshas. Due to its Tikta (bitter) and Kashaya (astringent) Rasa and Sheeta (cooling) Virya, it is the most prestigious herb for 'Yakṛt-shothahara' (hepatitis-curing) and 'Ashmarighna' (stone-breaking) therapies, clearing the fiery and clogging qualities of Pitta. It does not aggravate Vata when used appropriately.
Karma (Actions): Yakrit-shothahara (hepatitis-reliever), Mutra-janana (diuretic), Ashmarighna (stone-breaker), Jwarahara (antipyretic), Raktashodhaka (blood-purifier), and Vishaghna (anti-toxic).

API/AFI Adherence: The whole plant is the official medicinal part as per the Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India (API). It is the primary ingredient in iconic classical formulations like Bhumyamalaki Swarasa and various liver protective tonics.
Specific Indications: Used in Kamala (jaundice), Hepatitis B & C, Ashmari (kidney stones), Prameha (diabetes), and Shotha (generalized edema).
usageForm: Whole plant juice (Swarasa), Whole plant powder (Churna), Whole plant decoction (Kwatha), Medicated ghee (Ghrita).
mainMedicinalUses: World-Class Liver Support: Bhumyamalaki is the most researched natural remedy for Hepatitis B and C. It inhibits the replication of liver-damaging viruses and aids in the regeneration of hepatocytes. WHO-aligned research supports its role in reversing jaundice and liver enzymes imbalance.

Kidney Stone Breaker: Known as 'Stone Breaker' (Chanca Piedra in South America). It is the premier remedy for preventing and breaking kidney stones by inhibiting the formation of calcium oxalate crystals and promoting their excretion via urine.

Blood Cleansing and Skin: Its Raktashodhaka (blood-purifier) quality helps in clearing chronic skin conditions and toxins from the bloodstream. It is traditionally used to treat localized inflammation and skin rashes.
Diabetes and Metabolic Support: Its Tikta (bitter) and Kashaya (astringent) properties help in regulating blood sugar levels and improving insulin sensitivity in the body through metabolic balancing.

Nervous System Calm: Its Sheeta (cooling) quality provides a relaxing effect on the nervous system, helping to manage stress and anxiety related to Pitta imbalance. It is often used in traditional treatments for stress-induced high blood pressure.

Digestive and Spleen Care: Acts as an effective 'Pachana' (digestive) and 'Sangrahi' (absorbent), helping to clear 'Ama' (toxins) from the gut and managing spleen-related issues through its cooling action.

Phytochemical Profile: Rich in phyllanthin, hypophyllanthin, niranthin, phylltetralin, and various flavonoids that provide its multifaceted medicinal benefits, particularly its potent hepatoprotective and crystal-inhibiting properties.
plantProfile: Unique History: Classified under the 'Ashmarighna' (stone-clearing) and 'Yakrit-rakshaka' (liver-protecting) groups in ancient Ayurvedic treatises like Charaka Samhita. The name 'Bhumyamalaki' literally translates to 'Ground-Amla,' symbolizing its small stature and powerful life-supporting qualities similar to the Amla tree.

Physical Characteristics: A small, annual, erect herb growing up to 30-60 cm in height, usually found in moist, shady areas and as a common weed. It features thin, spreading branches and small, elliptical leaves arranged in two rows on branchlets, with tiny green flowers and fruits (pearl-like) hanging underneath the leaves. It is found throughout India and tropical regions worldwide.

Specific Chemical Compounds: Primarily contains Lignans (Phyllanthin, Hypophyllanthin), Flavonoids (Quercetin, Rutin), and Saponins. The presence of phyllanthin is crucial for its anti-viral and anti-inflammatory action on the liver cells.

Strict Standardizations: The whole plant is standardized in API based on macroscopic features (tiny fruits under leaves). Physicochemical benchmarks: total ash (<8%), alcohol-soluble extractive (>5%), and water-soluble extractive (>15%). HPTLC quantification is used to monitor phyllanthin content.
preparationMethods: Fresh Juice (Swarasa): 10-20ml of fresh juice taken twice daily with water or buttermilk for jaundice and liver protection.
Whole Plant Powder (Churna): 3-6g of fine powder taken twice daily with warm milk or honey for general vitality and blood purification.
Decoction (Kwatha): 20g of dried plant boiled in 160ml water reduced to 40ml for chronic kidney stone management and cooling.
Medicated Milk: 5-10g of powder boiled in milk; taken to support kidney health and manage diabetic thirst.
External Paste: Ground fresh plant or paste applied directly to skin rashes and localized swellings to reduce heat and inflammation.

dosages.Children: Generally safe. 2-5 ml of fresh juice mixed with honey twice daily for liver protection during childhood infections.
dosages.Adults: Powder: 3-6 grams; Swarasa (Juice): 10-20 ml; Decoction: 20-50 ml, divided throughout the day before meals.
dosages.Elderly: Excellent for age-related liver support and preventing stone formation. Dosage: 3g of powder daily with warm water at bedtime.

safetyProfile: Contraindications: Generally very safe. However, individuals with very low blood sugar should use it in moderation due to its potential hypoglycemic effect. Avoid during active extreme diarrhea episodes unless specifically prescribed.
GRAS Status: Classified as a safe medicinal herb with a long history of use across global traditional systems (Ayurveda, TCM, and Amazonian medicine) without documented toxicity.
Herb-Drug Interactions: Monitor if taken alongside synthetic anti-viral or anti-diabetic medications as it may potentiate their effects. No severe adverse interactions reported in WHO monographs.
Adverse Effects: None documented at traditional therapeutic levels. Excessive use in very sensitive individuals may rarely cause mild skin cooling or frequent urination.
